coredata.dc:title: New fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free silicon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Yanling
Liu, Yufei
Su, Chen
Ji, Xinghu
He, Zhike
coredata.dc:description: Silicon nanodots (SiNDs) are currently appealing to extensive studies due to their low toxicity and unique optical and electrical properties. In this work, we obtained label-free SiNDs via a facile hydrothermal method. The approach offers advantages in terms of cheap reactants, easy operation and a short time. The quantum yield (QY) of the SiNDs is up to 32.8%, and the as-prepared nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence (FL), higher photostability and obviously longer FL lifetime. More importantly, these label-free SiNDs can be used as a highly sensitive pH sensor, which FL respond rapidly to pH fluctuations and reveal a good linear relationship. Also, a superior pH-reversibility was demonstrated. Different salt ions show a little influence on the pH-responsive behavior. The pH of real water samples were tested on the basis of the proposed SiNDs sensor and the results are satisfactory. Further, traditional SiNDs with SiH or SiC surfaces make them less water-soluble or sensitive to the pH. Whereas, our SiNDs feature amino-terminated surface and these amino groups are conducive to the pH sensitivity. In a word, this new, cheap, low-toxic, and effective fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free SiNDs would be a promising candidate in biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanodots (SiNDs)
Hydrothermal method
Label-free
pH sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Real sample pH testing on the basis of SiNDs senor
The exploration of synthesis condition: molar ratio, temperature and time
The photostability and FL lifetime of SiNDs
Research on the pH response experiments
Real sample pH testing on the basis of SiNDs senor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized FL and UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared SiNDs. The photographs inset are SiNDs aqueous solution under visible and UV light, respectively.

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the as-prepared SiNDs. Inset in (a) is the TEM diameter distribution. Inset in (b) presents the enlarged HRTEM image of a single SiNP. (c) FTIR spectrum of as-prepared SiNDs. (d) EDX spectrum of as-prepared SiNDs. Inset in (d) the elemental ratios (weight and atom percentages) calculated by the EDX software.

Photostability of SiNDs. Three nanoparticles including SiNDs (λ em =448nm), red fluorescence QDs (Qr , λ em =623nm) and green fluorescence QDs (Qg , λ em =538nm) irradiated continuously by a Xe lamp (150W) for 64min.
The FL decay curves of (a) SiNDs (λ em =448nm) and (b) H33258 (λ em =460nm) and their corresponding lifetimes are 6.199, 3.771ns, respectively (λ ex =337nm).

(a and b) FL of SiNDs in BR buffer solutions at different pH values: 2.01, 3.02, 3.77, 4.02, 4.27, 4.52, 4.77, 5.02, 5.37, 5.77, 6.02, 7.77, 8.02, 8.55, 9.02, 10.02, 11.02. (c) FL response of different pH values: 3.52, 3.77, 4.02, 4.27, 4.52. Inset in (c) is the calibration curve from pH 3.52 to 4.52. (d) FL responses of the different pH values: 4.52, 4.77, 5.02, 5.37, 6.02, 6.77, 7.02, 7.77. Inset in (d) is the calibration curve from pH 4.52 to 7.77.

FL intensity of the SiNDs reversibly goes upward and downward by alternating the pH value of the solution. One cycle means that the FL intensity is measured as the value changed from pH 8.8 to 3.3 and then from pH 3.3 to 8.8.

FL intensity of SiNDs influenced by various salt ions in (a) CH3COOHCH3COONa buffer (150mM NaCl, pH 4.2) (b) Tris–HCl buffer solution (20mM Tris, 150mM NaCl, pH 8.0). The final salt ion concentration is 10mM.

pH values of different water samples (river water, rainwater, tap water, denized water, respectively.). Black bar is the calculated pH value according to the linearity equation (y =74.21x +120.79), red bar is the measured value form pH meter. Note: the river water is beyond the linear range (pH 4.52–7.77) in both calculated value and measured value, so it is not apply to this sensor and the calculated result of river water is incredible. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Synthesis high pH-sensitive SiNDs via hydrothermal method. (b) pH-sensitive SiNDs responds to pH fluctuations.

head.ce:title: New fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free silicon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon nanodots (SiNDs) are currently appealing to extensive studies due to their low toxicity and unique optical and electrical properties. In this work, we obtained label-free SiNDs via a facile hydrothermal method. The approach offers advantages in terms of cheap reactants, easy operation and a short time. The quantum yield (QY) of the SiNDs is up to 32.8%, and the as-prepared nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence (FL), higher photostability and obviously longer FL lifetime. More importantly, these label-free SiNDs can be used as a highly sensitive pH sensor, which FL respond rapidly to pH fluctuations and reveal a good linear relationship. Also, a superior pH-reversibility was demonstrated. Different salt ions show a little influence on the pH-responsive behavior. The pH of real water samples were tested on the basis of the proposed SiNDs sensor and the results are satisfactory. Further, traditional SiNDs with SiH or SiC surfaces make them less water-soluble or sensitive to the pH. Whereas, our SiNDs feature amino-terminated surface and these amino groups are conducive to the pH sensitivity. In a word, this new, cheap, low-toxic, and effective fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free SiNDs would be a promising candidate in biological field.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, intensive explorations have been focused on quantum dots (QDs) because of their unique optical and electrical properties [1–4]. Typical QDs, including II–VI (e.g. CdX, X=S, Se, Te) [5,6], III–V (e.g. InP, InAs, GaAs) [7,8] and IV–VI (e.g. PbX, X=S, Se) [9,10], have been applied in many fields and obtained excellent achievements. However, the toxicity of heavy metals in QDs still restricts their applications in biomedical field especially in vivo[11]. Therefore, low-toxic and biologically benign QDs are much more needed.

Unlike traditional QDs, SiNDs are intrinsically low-toxic and owns unique photoelectric properties. The FL properties of SiNDs are caused by a radiative recombination of electrons and holes [12]. SiNDs have shown significantly longer fluorescent lifetime compared with organic dyes [13]. Moreover, silicon is the second most abundant element on earth, so the reaction raw materials are relatively plentiful and inexpensive. In the past years, SiNDs have been synthesized by a variety of physical and chemical approaches, including etching silicon wafers [14,15], annealing SiO x powders followed by etching with HF [16], plasma synthesis [17], solution reaction of SiCl4, Mg2Si [18–21] and so on. Nevertheless, most resultant SiNDs are SiH terminated, which makes them susceptible to oxidation [22]. Besides, these SiNDs are less water-soluble or incompatible with hydrophilic solvents due to the SiH bonds on their surface [23,24]. Thus the native SiNDs have to be modified with hydrophilic ligands to render them water dispersible [23], which is inconvenient and time consuming.

Until now, much attention has been paid to fluorescent pH sensors for their widespread applications in the fields of chemical analysis, biomedicine, and environmental monitor and so on. For instance, they have been used in cellular biology for measuring intracellular pH and monitoring pH or pCO2 in blood [25]. Different kinds of fluorescent pH sensors including various kinds of QDs, silver nanoclusters and gold nanoparticles have been reported [26–28]. However, few researches have been focused on label-free (including undoped) fluorescent SiNDs. Herein, we introduce a highly sensitive and reversible fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free SiNDs. In the synthesis, (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) was used as the only silicon source and a facile hydrothermal method was employed. The as-prepared SiNDs show excellent optical performance including high QY (32.8%), higher photostability, and obviously longer FL lifetime compared with organic fluorescent dye. What's more important, compared with above-mentioned SiNDs, our SiNDs are amino-terminated, which made them well compatible with hydrophilic solvent without any modification. The amine groups in the surface not only make the SiNDs water-soluble, but also sensitive to pH changes. The sensor responds rapidly to pH fluctuations at the same time owns a superior reversibility. In addition, different salt ions have shown a little influence on the pH-responsive behavior. Therefore, a cheap, low-toxic, effective and reversible fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free SiNDs was demonstrated for the first time.

APTMS (97%) was purchased from Aladdin, trisodium citrate (99%) and Calf Thymus DNA were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich, Hoechst 33258 (H33258) was purchased from Invitrogen. quinine sulfate, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, CdCl2·2.5H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NaNO3, Na2SO4, Na3PO4·12H2O were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich or Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Britton-Robinson (BR) buffers (pH 1.81–11.98) were prepared by mixing 0.2M NaOH and a mixture of 0.04M H3PO4, H3BO3, and CH3COOH according to suitable proportion. The buffer used were CH3COOH–CH3COONa buffer (pH 4.2, 150mM NaCl) and 20mM Tris–HCl buffer solution (pH 8.0, 150mM NaCl). Water-soluble CdTe:Zn2+ quantum dots were prepared according to the literature [29]. The pH values of solutions were measured with a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter. All chemicals used were of analytical grade or of the highest purity available unless otherwise stated and used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (Millipore) as the solvent unless otherwise stated. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired with a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV/visible spectrophotometer. FL spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 spectrofluorometer. FL intensity decay curves were measured on QM/TM system (Photon Technology International). FTIR spectra over the range 400–4000cm−1 were performed on a Nicolet Avatar-360 FTIR spectrometer. EDX spectra were captured by a FEI Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer. TEM sample was prepared by dropping an aqueous SiNDs solution onto Agar carbon-coated copper grids (400 meshes) with the excess solvent evaporated. TEM image was obtained with a JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscope. The quantum yield of SiNDs was measured according to literature and quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY=58%) was chosen as the reference standard [30]. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The preparation of SiNDs via hydrothermal route was carried out as follows. In a typical procedure, 0.368g trisodium citrate was dissolved in 8ml deionized water in a flask with sustainable aeration of nitrogen for 20min to remove oxygen. Then 2ml APTMS was injected into the nitrogen-saturated precursor solution and homogeneously stirred for 10min. Then, the solution was loaded into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and incubated in an oven at 200°C for 2h and cooled to room temperature by a hydro-cooling process. To remove the excess impurities including APTMS and trisodium citrate at the end of the preparation, 1kDa dialysis bag was used to purify the solution and the as-prepared SiNDs solution was collected and stored at 4°C for use.

Briefly, the as-prepared SiNDs solution was diluted 12 times using deionized water. And, 20μL SiNDs, 980μL BR buffer solutions of different pH values were mixed together and stayed for 10min, and then the FL spectra of the mixtures were recorded. The excitation wavelength was 359nm in all of the experiments in this paper unless otherwise stated.

Typically, as-prepared SiNDs solution was diluted 12 times using deionized water, and 20μL SiNDs, 100μL different salt solutions (0.1M), 880μL Tris–HCl buffer or CH3COOH–CH3COONa buffer were mixed together and stayed for 10min, then the FL spectra of the mixtures were recorded.

As-prepared SiNDs solution was diluted 12 times using deionized water, and 500ml SiNDs were transfered to the beaker. Then 1M HCl was used to adjust the pH of the SiNDs solution to make the pH change from pH 8.8 to 3.3, and then the FL spectra were recorded. After that, 1M NaOH was used to adjust the pH of the SiNDs solution to make the pH change from pH 3.3 to 8.8 and recorded the FL spectra. The same operations were made for four rounds. And a big volume (500ml) of the SiNDs is to avoid the whole volume change with the little added HCl and NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample pH testing on the basis of SiNDs senor
ce:section.ce:para: Four water samples including river water, rainwater, tap water and denized water were filtrated before measurement. As-prepared SiNDs solution was diluted 12 times using deionized water 20μL SiNDs, 980μL water sample were mixtured and stayed for 10min and then the FL spectra of the mixtures were recorded. With the calibration of curve (pH 4.52–7.77), the pH values of the sample solution were determined. At the same time, the measured pH values of the samples were tested by pH meter.

Preparation of the SiNDs sensor is shown in Scheme 1a. APTMS serves as the silicon source and trisodium citrate is deemed as a protective agent in this preparation. After mixing the two with a certain ratio in the absence of oxygen, the reaction was carried out in autoclave and incubated in an oven at 200°C for 2h under the optimal conditions. Scheme 1b depicts the SiNDs sensor responds rapidly to pH fluctuations. Nitrogen containing organic compounds, especially those with short chains, are often used as effective passivating agents [31–33], there has report that if one or more amine groups were present, the QY of nanopaticles will be over 10% [34]. So in the experiments, the amine moieties protonates to ammonium salt (−NH3 +) at low pH, the passivation effective of the amino group may be susceptible. Also, the SiNDs have been proved to contain SiO bond, according to the literature, silicon nanoparticle-to-alkoxy ligand SiO chemical bond is susceptible to low pH hydrolysis [34], so the nanodots may be influenced and the FL intensity is decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The exploration of synthesis condition: molar ratio, temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: Since molar ratio of reactants, reaction temperature and reaction time can influence the quality of the prepared SiNDs, experimental conditions must be strictly controlled to obtain high-quality SiNDs. We explored the influence of APTMS: trisodium citrate mole ratios, with mere alteration the mole ratio of the two reactants (reaction time: 2h, reaction temperature: 200°C), we found that the difference is slight on the FL spectra (all the resultant SiNDs solutions were adjusted to the same intensity in UV to insure a same concentration), as shown in Fig. S1. By controlling the reaction temperature (160–233°C) and reaction time (1–3h), we obtained optimization condition to obtain SiNDs solution, as depicted in Fig. S2 and S3. The maximum emission wavelength is 359nm, hence we chose 359nm as the excitation wavelength. Particularly, with the optimized conditions obtained from Figs. S1–S3 at reactants mole ratio (APTMS: Trisodium citrate is 9:1), reaction temperature 200°C and reaction time 2h, the 32.8% QY of the as-prepared SiNDs with quinine sulfate (QY=58%) as a reference standard was achieved.

Fig. 1 presents the normalized UV-FL spectra of the resultant SiNDs with the maximum emission wavelength (λ em =448nm), inserts are photographs of SiNDs under visible and UV lights, respectively. Obviously, the as-prepared SiNP solution has clearly resolved absorption peaks and symmetrical fluorescent peaks and good optical properties. This high quality is due to quantum-size effect and the high probability of producing a defect-free structure of this size (less than 5nm) [35].

The morphology of the as-prepared SiNDs was studied by TEM. A typical image for the prepared SiNDs was displayed in Fig. 2 . The size distribution is shown in Fig. 2(a and b), it is quite evident that the size of the spherical SiNDs is uniform with an excellent monodispersity. The average diameter of the nanoparticles is around 2.5nm. The inset in Fig. 2b is the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of SiNDs, which shows its high crystallinity. In addition, in order to confirm the surface structure of the SiNDs, the FTIR spectra was measured in Fig. 2c. The features between 920 and 1200cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational stretching of SiO molecular components [22,36]. The strong absorbance at 1384, 1577, and 3423cm−1 are, assigned to the CO and NH bending vibrations and the NH stretching vibration, respectively [37,38]. The result is similar with that synthesized by the microwave heating method [39] and indicate that amino groups are on the surface. The EDX technique was employed to probe a semi-quantitative picture on the surface composition of the SiNDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, the EDX result displays that the SiNDs contain Si, O, and N of 7.22, 27.19 and 11.19% at concentration, respectively. Whereas the C can be partly come from the carbon-coated copper grids thus is incredible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photostability and FL lifetime of SiNDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To test the photostability of the prepared SiNDs, We compared the SiNDs with the red fluorescence QDs (Q
r , λ em =623nm) and green fluorescence QDs (Q g, λ em =538nm). So these nanopaticle solutions with different emission wavelength in ambient atmosphere were irradiated continuously by a Xe lamp (150W) for 64min. The results are shown in Fig. 3 , which indicate that both Q
r and the SiNDs are approximately stabilized within 30min. The shapely increase of FL in SiNDs at the beginning is probably because of “photoactivation” or known as photobrightening, a luminescence enhancement produced when QDs are irradiated with UV–vis light [40].

The FL lifetime of SiNDs was also tested, we compared the SiNDs with fluorescent dye H33258. H33258 binding to double-stranded DNA will emit strong blue light [41]. Fig. 4 displays the time resolved FL decay curves of (a) SiNDs and (b) H33258 and their corresponding lifetimes are 6.199, 3.771ns, respectively. Obviously, the lifetime of SiNDs is notably longer than the commercial fluorescent dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on the pH response experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The standard sensing action of SiNDs was characterized by FL in BR buffer solutions from pH 2.01 to 11.02. As shown in Fig. 5(a and b), the SiNDs are highly sensitive to the pH adjusted by BR buffer solutions. The FL intensity reaches the maximum value and keeps stable at pH 7.77. Quantitatively, Fig. 5(c and d) exhibits representative FL responses with the different pH values. The insets are linear dependence between the FL intensity and pH. The linear range are pH 3.52–4.52, pH 4.52–7.77 and the linearity equation are y =288.95x–832.05 (R 2 =0.99), y =74.21x +120.79 (R 2 =0.98), respectively. The pH-responsive ranges of the reported nanoscaled pH sensors are different, such as pH 7.1–8.5 for CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs, pH 6.0–9.0 for CdTe QDs, pH 4.0–8.0 for mercaptoacetic acid-capped CdSe/ZnSe/ZnS QDs, pH 6.0–8.0 for glutathione-coated CdSe/CdZnS QDs, and pH 2.0–5.5 for DNA-modified gold nanoparticles mediated by i-motif [27]. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first report about pH-sensitive and label-free SiNDs. In addition, the inflection point was at pH 4.52. We assumed that when the proton increased to this point, there possibly exists a situation that the proton reaction rate reached to balanced state and led to this point.

The pH-reversibility of the SiNDs was shown in Fig. 6 . FL intensity of the SiNDs reversibly goes upward and downward in four cycles, which indicated the high pH-reversibility. Finally, we investigated the influences of different ions on this pH sensor. The final results were displayed in Fig. 7 . In two different buffers (pH 4.2 and pH 8.0) The SiNDs dissolved with different salt ions do not show obviously difference compared with the SiNDs dissolved without salt ions, which showed a good property of anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample pH testing on the basis of SiNDs senor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the proposed sensor, pH values of different water samples including river water, rainwater, tap water and deionized water were tested on the basis of SiNDs senor. The final results were displayed in Fig. 8 . The calculated values are pH 8.12 for river water, pH 6.77 for rainwater, pH 7.32 for tap water, pH 6.38 for deionized water, the measured values are pH 7.94, pH 6.78, pH 7.46, pH 6.82, accordingly. The results of the calculated values are very close to the measured values by pH meter. Note: the pH of river water is beyond the linear range (pH 4.52–7.77) in both calculated value and measured value, so it is not apply to this sensor and the calculated pH value of river water is incredible.

In this work, we demonstrated a new fluorescent pH sensor based on label-free silicon nanodots prepared by hydrothermal method. The approach is simple and effective, and the as-prepared nanoparticles exhibit good optical properties. What is important, these SiNDs are pH-sensitive, and they feature an excellent pH-reversibility and anti-interference. In conclusion, all the above experimental results reveal that this kind of luminescent SiNDs can be an excellent candidate for pH sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Scientific Program Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (2011CB933600), the Suzhou Nanotechnology Special Project (ZXG2013028), the National Science Foundation of China (21275109 and 21205089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.07.050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Highly efficient multiple exciton generation in colloidal PbSe and PbS quantum dots

Dynamics of porous silicon formation by etching in HF+V2O5 solutions
Enhanced photoluminescence from Si nano-organosols by functionalization with alkenes and their size evolution
Silicon nanocrystals with ensemble quantum yields exceeding 60%
Size and spectroscopy of silicon nanoparticles prepared via reduction of SiCl4
Solution reduction synthesis of surface stabilized silicon nanoparticles
Solution synthesis of ultrastable luminescent siloxane-coated silicon nanoparticles
Solution synthesis of alkyl- and alkyl/alkoxy-capped silicon nanoparticles via oxidation of Mg2Si
Water-soluble silicon quantum dots with wavelength-tunable photoluminescence
Highly luminescent and nontoxic amine-capped nanoparticles from porous silicon: synthesis and their use in biomedical imaging
Silica cross-linked micelles loading with silicon nanoparticles: preparation and characterization
A fluorescence ratiometric nano-pH sensor based on dual-fluorophore-doped silica nanoparticles
Green and orange CdTe quantum dots as effective ph-sensitive fluorescent probes for dual simultaneous and independent detection of viruses
Highly sensitive fluorescent and colorimetric pH sensor based on polyethylenimine-capped silver nanoclusters
pH effect on surface potential of polyelectrolytes-capped gold nanoparticles probed by surface-enhanced Raman scattering
One-pot synthesis of DNA-CdTe: Zn2+ nanocrystals using Na2TeO3 as the Te source
Microwave assisted one-step green synthesis of cell-permeable multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots without surface passivation reagents
Using liquid and solid state NMR and photoluminescence to study the synthesis and solubility properties of amine capped silicon nanoparticles
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
Amine-terminated silicon nanoparticles: synthesis, optical properties and their use in bioimaging
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Luminescent colloidal dispersion of silicon quantum dots from microwave plasma synthesis: exploring the photoluminescence behavior across the visible spectrum
Photo and pH stable, highly-luminescent silicon nanospheres and their bioconjugates for immunofluorescent cell imaging
Covalent attachment and derivatization of poly(l-lysine) monolayers on gold surfaces as characterized by polarization-modulation FT-IR spectroscopy
Electrocatalytic reduction of nitrite ion on a toluidine blue sol–gel thin film electrode derived from 3-aminopropyl trimethoxy silane
Large-scale aqueous synthesis of fluorescent and biocompatible silicon nanoparticles and their use as highly photostable biological probes
Quantum dots luminescence enhancement due to illumination with UV/Vis light
Improved fluorometric DNA determination based on the interaction of the dna/polycation complex with Hoechst 33258

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanling Feng is currently a master degree student in Wuhan University. She works on the development of novel fluorescent nano materials and fluorescent sensors.
Yufei Liu is currently a Ph.D. student in Wuhan University. He works on molecular spectroscopy analysis and chemical sensors now.
Chen Su is currently a master degree student in Wuhan University. Her research interest is biosensor based on DNA molecular light switches.

Xinghu Ji received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2007. He is currently a Lecturer at Wuhan University. His research interest focuses on microfluidics and application in analytical chemistry for biology and medicine.

Zhike He is a Professor of Analytical Chemistry at Wuhan University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 1995. His research interest covers Nano-bio-analytical chemistry and application for biology and medicine.
